does: This paper introduces a framework for controlling advanced AI systems using weak supervisors—models or humans with limited capabilities compared to the systems being trained. The core contribution is demonstrating that deep learning models exhibit weak-to-strong generalization, where a strong model can learn from imperfect supervision provided by a weaker model and generalize beyond those weak labels to achieve stronger performance. The authors provide initial empirical evidence that this approach works across various domains and model scales, suggesting it may offer a practical method for aligning increasingly capable AI systems without requiring equally capable supervisors.
connects: This work builds on longstanding concerns about the alignment problem in AI safety, particularly the challenge of superalignment—ensuring that superintelligent systems remain controllable by human overseers. It extends classical ideas about learning from imperfect labels and noisy supervision, connecting to recent work on distillation and learning from weaker models. The research responds to the practical limitation that human feedback becomes a bottleneck as AI systems surpass human capabilities in specialized domains, building on recent progress in reinforcement learning from human feedback and scalable oversight approaches while offering a fundamentally different direction grounded in the empirical properties of neural network generalization.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward practical superalignment by suggesting that weak supervisors may suffice for controlling strong models, potentially scaling oversight to increasingly capable systems. It enables research into characterizing and improving weak-to-strong generalization across diverse domains and model architectures. Future work could explore theoretical foundations explaining when and why this generalization occurs, develop methods to optimize the transfer from weak to strong models, investigate whether humans can serve effectively as weak supervisors for superhuman AI systems, and test the approach on progressively more challenging alignment problems where the capability gap between supervisor and model widens significantly.
